class_schedule.topic: Mobile Platform Differences: iOS and Android
learning_objectives: Compare iOS and Android hardware architectures and their impact on game development
Understand mobile GPU rendering models and how they differ from desktop GPUs
Identify the key constraints of mobile development: memory, battery, thermal, and storage
Configure Unity for dual-platform mobile development
readings: Beginning Mobile Game Development Ch. 1-2
Apple Human Interface Guidelines - Game Design
Android Game Developer Documentation - Platform Overview
ARM GPU Best Practices Developer Guide Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Mobile Game Development. Mobile gaming generates more revenue than console and PC gaming combined, but developing for mobile requires a fundamentally different mindset. Desktop game developers have gigabytes of RAM, dedicated GPUs with hundreds of watts of thermal headroom, and precise keyboard-mouse input. Mobile developers have constrained memory (2-6GB shared between CPU and GPU), tile-based GPUs that minimize memory bandwidth at the expense of different performance characteristics, battery-powered devices that throttle under sustained load, and imprecise touch input on a screen that your thumbs occlude. These constraints are not limitations to work around -- they are the defining characteristics of the platform. A great mobile game is not a desktop game made smaller; it is a game designed from the ground up for the way people use phones and tablets. Today we survey the iOS and Android hardware landscape, examine the tile-based deferred rendering architecture used by mobile GPUs (Apple GPU, Adreno, Mali), discuss the fragmentation challenge on Android versus the controlled ecosystem of iOS, and set up Unity for building to both platforms simultaneously.
key_concepts: iOS hardware ecosystem: A-series and M-series chips, Metal API, controlled device matrix (iPhone/iPad models)
Android hardware ecosystem: Qualcomm Snapdragon, Samsung Exynos, MediaTek Dimensity, Vulkan and OpenGL ES APIs
Tile-based deferred rendering (TBDR): mobile GPUs process the screen in small tiles to minimize memory bandwidth
Android fragmentation: thousands of device configurations with varying GPU capabilities, screen sizes, and OS versions
Memory constraints: typical 2-4GB RAM shared between system and GPU, aggressive OS memory pressure and app termination
Battery and thermal budgets: sustained high-performance workloads cause thermal throttling and battery drain
Storage considerations: initial download size limits (200MB for cellular), expandable storage on Android, SSD vs eMMC speeds
Display diversity: aspect ratios from 16:9 to 21:9, refresh rates from 60Hz to 120Hz, notches and punch-holes
Unity mobile build targets: iOS (Xcode required, ARM64 only), Android (SDK, NDK, Gradle, ARM64 and ARMv7)
Minimum spec targeting: defining the lowest hardware tier you support and designing scalable quality settings
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a Unity project with both iOS and Android build targets configured, demonstrating the differences in Player Settings for each platform, building and deploying to a physical Android device, and running the Unity Profiler over USB to observe the baseline frame time breakdown on mobile hardware.
setup_requirements: Unity 2023.2 LTS with iOS and Android build support modules installed
Android Studio with SDK 33+ and NDK installed
Xcode 15+ on macOS for iOS builds
Physical Android device with USB debugging enabled, or physical iOS device with developer provisioning

review.value: Mobile GPUs divide the screen into small tiles (16x16 or 32x32 pixels) and render each tile entirely in on-chip memory before writing the result to main memory once. This dramatically reduces memory bandwidth but means overdraw is especially expensive.

Over 24,000 distinct Android device models with different GPUs, screen sizes, OS versions, and performance levels. Requires defining a minimum spec, testing on representative devices across GPU vendors, and implementing scalable quality settings.

After 5-15 minutes of sustained high GPU/CPU load, mobile devices reduce clock speeds to prevent overheating. A game that runs at 60fps initially may drop to 30fps after extended play if it runs too close to the hardware's thermal limit.

Unlike desktop systems with dedicated VRAM, mobile devices share a single pool of RAM between CPU and GPU. A texture loaded for rendering directly competes with memory used for game logic, audio, and the operating system.

learning_objectives: Design intuitive touch-based control schemes for different game genres
Implement multi-touch gesture recognition for tap, swipe, pinch, and rotate
Apply ergonomic principles to virtual joystick and button placement
Handle input conflicts when multiple touch gestures overlap
readings: Touch Interaction Design for Games Ch. 3-5
Unity Input System Documentation - Touch Support
Apple HIG - Touch and Gestures
Material Design - Touch Mechanics

lecture_notes.introduction: Touch input is fundamentally different from keyboard and mouse. Your thumbs occlude the bottom third of the screen, precision is measured in millimeters not pixels, there is no hover state, and players expect gesture-based interactions that feel native to the device. A mobile game that simply maps keyboard controls to on-screen buttons will always feel inferior to a game designed around touch from the start. The best mobile games -- Brawl Stars, Monument Valley, Geometry Dash -- use control schemes that feel natural on a touchscreen and would be worse on any other input device. Today we study the touch event lifecycle (began, moved, stationary, ended, canceled), design ergonomic control layouts based on thumb reach zones, implement a virtual joystick with dead zones and edge clamping, build a gesture recognizer that handles simultaneous tap-swipe-pinch without conflicts, and add haptic feedback to confirm touch interactions. We will work with Unity's new Input System, which provides a unified API for touch, gamepad, and keyboard input that simplifies cross-platform development. By the end of this session, you should be able to design a touch control scheme for any game genre and implement it with proper dead zones, gesture disambiguation, and haptic feedback.
key_concepts: Touch event lifecycle: TouchPhase.Began, Moved, Stationary, Ended, Canceled and their frame timing
Thumb ergonomics: comfortable reach zones for left and right thumbs in portrait and landscape orientations
Virtual joystick design: static vs dynamic positioning, dead zone radius (10-15% of stick range), edge behavior
Gesture recognition: tap (short touch with minimal movement), swipe (directional movement above velocity threshold), pinch/rotate (two-finger)
Gesture disambiguation: priority systems and timing windows to resolve conflicts between tap and swipe, or single-tap and double-tap
Haptic feedback: UIImpactFeedbackGenerator on iOS, Vibrator API on Android, triggering on button press and collision events
Fitts's Law on mobile: minimum touch target size of 44pt (iOS) / 48dp (Android) for reliable tapping
Safe area handling: adjusting UI layout for notches, rounded corners, and home indicator bars
Input buffering for touch: queuing rapid touch inputs to prevent dropped inputs during high-action moments
Accessibility: supporting alternative input methods, adjustable control sizes, and one-handed play options
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a complete touch input system for a top-down action game: a dynamic virtual joystick on the left side for movement (with dead zone and smooth return), a tap-to-shoot mechanic on the right side, swipe-to-dodge that coexists with the shoot tap without conflict, and haptic feedback on shooting and taking damage. Testing on a physical device to verify thumb reach comfort.
setup_requirements: Unity project with Input System package installed and configured for touch
Physical mobile device for testing touch ergonomics
UI Canvas configured with CanvasScaler for multiple screen sizes
Simple game character for demonstrating input response

review.value: A circular region at the center of the joystick where input is ignored, preventing jitter from imprecise thumb placement. Typically 10-15% of the joystick radius. Without a dead zone, the character drifts when the player rests their thumb on the stick.

The process of determining which gesture the player intended when multiple gestures could match the same touch sequence. For example, a tap requires waiting a short delay (150-200ms) to confirm it is not the start of a swipe.

A virtual joystick that appears wherever the player first touches the screen rather than being fixed in one position. Accommodates different hand sizes and grip positions. Disappears when the finger is lifted.

The rectangular region of the screen not obscured by device hardware (notches, rounded corners, home indicators). All interactive UI elements must be placed within the safe area. Unity provides Screen.safeArea to query the safe rectangle.

learning_objectives: Apply mobile-native UI design patterns to game interfaces
Implement responsive layouts that adapt to diverse screen sizes and aspect ratios
Design onboarding flows that teach game mechanics without interrupting play
Evaluate mobile game UI against platform-specific usability guidelines
readings: Mobile Game Design Essentials Ch. 6-8
Apple Human Interface Guidelines - UI Components
Material Design 3 - Game UI Patterns
Nielsen Norman Group: Mobile Game Usability Heuristics

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile game UI must accomplish everything desktop game UI does while contending with smaller screens, touch-only input, and players who give you about 30 seconds before deciding whether to keep playing or uninstall. Every tap should produce immediate visual feedback. Every menu should be reachable within two taps from gameplay. Every piece of information the player needs should be visible without memorizing icon meanings. Mobile players are often in environments with distractions -- on a bus, waiting in line, on a couch with the TV on -- so your UI must be self-explanatory and forgiving. Today we study the UI conventions that successful mobile games have established: bottom-aligned navigation for thumb accessibility, modal sheets that slide up from the bottom rather than dialog boxes, progress indicators that motivate continued play, currency and shop interfaces that are clear about what costs real money, and onboarding sequences that teach through doing rather than telling. We implement a complete game HUD with health bar, score, and ability cooldowns that adapts to screen sizes from a 5-inch phone to a 12.9-inch iPad, respects safe areas, and provides clear visual feedback for every player action.
key_concepts: Mobile UI hierarchy: gameplay HUD (always visible), menus (one tap away), settings (two taps away), deep settings (three taps)
Bottom navigation pattern: primary actions within thumb reach at screen bottom, consistent across the app
Responsive layout with anchors: UI elements positioned relative to screen edges and safe areas, not absolute coordinates
Canvas Scaler strategies: Scale With Screen Size for consistent proportions, or Constant Physical Size for touch targets
Onboarding through play: progressive disclosure of mechanics through guided early-game sequences, not tutorial text screens
Visual feedback principles: every tap produces a response within 100ms (animation, sound, haptic), every state change is animated
Currency display conventions: clear distinction between premium (paid) and soft (earned) currencies, confirmation before real-money purchases
Notification badges and progression indicators: red dots for new content, progress bars for motivation, reward previews
Localization considerations: UI layouts that accommodate text expansion (German is 30% longer than English), RTL language support
Accessibility features: colorblind-safe palettes, scalable text, voiceover support, reduced motion options
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete mobile game HUD: health bar with animated damage and healing, score counter with punch animation on increase, ability buttons with cooldown radial fill, and a pause menu that slides up from the bottom with volume controls and quit confirmation. Testing the layout on phone (16:9), modern phone (19.5:9 with notch), and tablet (4:3) aspect ratios to verify responsive behavior.
setup_requirements: Unity project with UI Toolkit or Canvas-based UI system
Device simulator with multiple screen configurations
UI sprite atlas for game HUD elements
DOTween or similar animation library for UI transitions

review.value: Introducing game mechanics one at a time through gameplay rather than front-loading all information. Level 1 teaches movement, Level 2 teaches combat, Level 3 teaches abilities. Each mechanic is learned by doing, not by reading.

UI elements are anchored to screen edges (top-left, bottom-center, etc.) with percentage-based offsets rather than fixed pixel positions. When the screen size changes, elements maintain their relative positions and appropriate spacing.

Player actions must produce visible feedback within 100ms to feel responsive. For touch: immediate color change on touch-down, animation on touch-up. For gameplay: immediate visual and audio acknowledgment of every input even before the game state resolves.

Real-money transactions require explicit confirmation dialogs, clear display of what the player receives, and a separate visual treatment from free currencies. Platform guidelines require specific disclosure language for purchases.

learning_objectives: Establish and enforce performance budgets for mobile game subsystems
Profile mobile games using Unity Profiler, Xcode Instruments, and Android GPU Inspector
Identify and resolve CPU, GPU, and memory bottlenecks on mobile hardware
Implement thermal monitoring and adaptive quality scaling
readings: Unity Mobile Optimization Guide
ARM GPU Best Practices Developer Guide Ch. 4-6
Apple Developer Documentation - MetricKit
Android Performance Patterns - Memory Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Performance is not optional on mobile. A game that drains the battery in 45 minutes will be uninstalled. A game that makes the phone uncomfortably hot will be uninstalled. A game that stutters during critical gameplay moments will receive a one-star review. Mobile performance optimization starts with establishing budgets: how many milliseconds does each subsystem get per frame, how many megabytes of memory can you use, and how many watts of power can you sustain without triggering thermal throttling. These budgets must be set before development begins and enforced throughout production with automated profiling. Today we cover the complete mobile profiling toolkit: Unity's built-in Profiler for CPU and GPU timing over USB, Xcode Instruments for Metal GPU trace and energy impact on iOS, Android GPU Inspector for Vulkan/GLES analysis, and Unity Memory Profiler for tracking managed and native heap usage. We profile a sample game that runs fine in the editor but drops to 25fps on a mid-range phone, identify the specific bottlenecks (too many draw calls, uncompressed textures consuming 400MB, GC spikes from per-frame allocations), and fix each one with measurable improvement. We also implement a thermal monitoring system that detects when the device is getting hot and proactively reduces quality settings to maintain frame rate.
key_concepts: Frame budget at 60fps: 16.67ms total, typically 8-10ms rendering, 2ms physics, 2ms scripts, 2ms headroom
Memory budget: 300-500MB for a typical mobile game on a device with 3-4GB total RAM (OS takes 1-2GB)
Draw call budget: 100-200 draw calls on mid-range devices, 50-100 on low-end, with batching to consolidate
Unity Profiler over USB: connecting to a device build for real CPU/GPU frame timing and GC allocation tracking
Xcode Instruments: Metal GPU trace for per-draw-call GPU timing, Energy Log for power consumption measurement
Android GPU Inspector: frame-by-frame GPU workload analysis, shader profiling, counter collection
GC allocation detection: identifying per-frame allocations that cause periodic GC pauses (string operations, LINQ, boxing)
Texture memory analysis: identifying uncompressed or oversized textures using Unity Memory Profiler
Thermal state monitoring: using iOS NSProcessInfo.thermalState or Android PowerManager to detect throttling
Adaptive quality scaling: dynamically adjusting render resolution, shadow quality, particle count, and LOD distance based on frame time or thermal state
lecture_notes.demonstration: Profiling a sample mobile game on a physical device: identifying that it is GPU-bound at 24ms/frame due to 380 draw calls, finding 3 uncompressed 2048x2048 textures consuming 192MB, and detecting a 4ms GC spike every 2 seconds from string concatenation in the score display. Fixing each issue (enabling static batching, switching to ASTC compression, caching the score string) and verifying the game now runs at a stable 60fps with 280MB memory usage.
setup_requirements: Physical mobile device connected via USB for profiling
Unity Profiler configured for remote connection to device builds
Sample game build with intentional performance problems
Unity Memory Profiler package installed

review.value: When per-frame allocations (string concatenation, LINQ, new arrays) accumulate enough garbage, the GC pauses the game thread for 2-10ms to collect it. On mobile, this causes visible frame drops. Prevent by pre-allocating, pooling, and using StringBuilder.

Adaptive Scalable Texture Compression offers variable block sizes from 4x4 (highest quality) to 12x12 (highest compression). A 2048x2048 RGBA texture is 16MB uncompressed but only 2-4MB with ASTC 6x6. Supported on all modern iOS and Android GPUs.

The sustained power draw a device can handle without throttling. Running at maximum GPU utilization continuously will exceed the thermal budget within minutes. Target 70-80% GPU utilization to allow headroom for thermal stability.

A runtime system that monitors frame time or thermal state and adjusts quality settings dynamically. When frame time exceeds budget, reduce render resolution, shadow quality, or particle count. When frame time is well under budget, increase quality.

learning_objectives: Optimize rendering for tile-based mobile GPU architectures
Implement draw call reduction through batching, atlasing, and instancing
Select appropriate texture compression formats for iOS and Android
Design shader complexity tiers for different mobile hardware generations
readings: Unity URP Mobile Rendering Documentation
Qualcomm Adreno GPU Performance Guide
Apple Metal Best Practices Guide - Rendering
GPU Pro 7 Ch. 5 - Mobile Rendering Techniques

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile GPUs are not small desktop GPUs. They use a fundamentally different rendering architecture -- tile-based deferred rendering -- that processes the screen in small tiles using fast on-chip memory to minimize bandwidth to the power-hungry main memory. This architecture makes certain operations cheap (depth testing, alpha blending within a tile) and others expensive (reading from framebuffer textures, overdraw that fills multiple tiles). Understanding this architecture is the key to writing performant mobile shaders and scene setups. Today we focus on the rendering-specific optimizations that make the difference between 30fps and 60fps on mobile: reducing draw calls through static batching and sprite atlasing, minimizing overdraw with proper sorting and alpha-test alternatives to alpha-blend, choosing the right texture compression format for each platform (ASTC for both modern platforms, ETC2 for legacy Android), writing mobile-friendly shaders that avoid expensive operations (dependent texture reads, excessive branching, high register pressure), and leveraging URP's built-in mobile optimizations (single-pass instanced rendering, SRP Batcher). We will profile each optimization to quantify its impact on real mobile hardware.
key_concepts: TBDR optimization: minimize overdraw (sorted rendering), avoid framebuffer fetches, use tile-local operations
Draw call reduction: static batching for non-moving UI, SRP Batcher for varied materials, GPU instancing for repeated meshes
Texture atlasing: packing multiple small textures into one large texture to enable batching of objects with different sprites
Alpha blending vs alpha testing: alpha-blended objects prevent early-Z optimization; alpha test (cutout) allows it but has edge aliasing
ASTC texture compression: universal on modern iOS and Android, variable block sizes for quality/size tradeoff per texture
ETC2 compression: OpenGL ES 3.0 mandatory format, useful for legacy Android devices lacking ASTC support
Mobile shader complexity: avoid pow(), log(), sqrt() chains; minimize texture samples; prefer half-precision (float16) where possible
URP mobile features: 2D Renderer for 2D games, Forward Renderer with single main light, disabled features for mobile profiles
Render scale: rendering at 80-90% resolution and upscaling to native, saving 20-35% fragment shading cost with minimal visual impact
Overdraw visualization: using URP's overdraw debug view to identify overlapping transparent elements that waste fill rate
lecture_notes.demonstration: Taking a mobile scene from 400 draw calls and 22ms frame time to under 100 draw calls and 10ms: combining UI sprites into an atlas, enabling SRP Batcher for 3D materials, replacing alpha-blended foliage with alpha-tested cutout, switching textures from uncompressed to ASTC 6x6, and enabling 85% render scale with bilinear upscale. Measuring each change independently with the profiler.
setup_requirements: Unity URP project with a mobile game scene
Physical mobile device for profiling
Sprite atlas tool for UI texture packing
URP Rendering Debugger for overdraw visualization

review.value: Unity's Scriptable Render Pipeline batching system that reduces the CPU cost of setting up draw calls by caching shader property data on the GPU. Unlike static batching, it works with different meshes as long as they share the same shader variant.

Rendering the scene at a lower resolution than the display's native resolution and upscaling the result. At 85% scale, you render 72% as many pixels (0.85 x 0.85), saving significant fragment shading cost. The quality loss is often imperceptible on mobile screens.

Using 16-bit floating-point (half/mediump) instead of 32-bit (float/highp) in shaders where full precision is not needed (colors, UVs, most lighting). Doubles the ALU throughput on many mobile GPUs and reduces register pressure.

Each additional layer of transparent geometry at a pixel requires the fragment shader to run again within the tile. On mobile, where fill rate is limited, overlapping transparent particles or UI elements can easily become the primary bottleneck.

learning_objectives: Evaluate premium, freemium, ad-supported, and hybrid monetization strategies
Design ethical monetization that respects player experience and regulatory requirements
Implement in-app purchase flows with server-side receipt validation
Analyze key business metrics: ARPDAU, LTV, CPI, and conversion funnels
readings: Free-to-Play: Making Money From Games You Give Away Ch. 3-7
Apple StoreKit 2 Documentation
Google Play Billing Library 6.0 Documentation
FTC Guidelines on Mobile Game Monetization (2024)

lecture_notes.introduction: A great mobile game that nobody can sustain financially is a failed product. Understanding monetization is as important as understanding rendering optimization, because both determine whether the game survives. The mobile market has converged on several proven models: premium (pay once, own forever), freemium (free to play with optional purchases), ad-supported (free with ads as the revenue source), and hybrid approaches that combine these elements. Each model has profound implications for game design -- a premium game optimizes for initial purchase conversion, while a freemium game optimizes for long-term retention and recurring spending. Today we examine each model's economics, design principles, and ethical boundaries. Freemium in particular requires careful design to avoid predatory mechanics: loot boxes with undisclosed odds, pay-to-win advantages in competitive games, and dark patterns that pressure children into purchases have rightfully drawn regulatory scrutiny worldwide. We will implement a complete IAP flow using Apple StoreKit 2 and Google Play Billing Library through Unity's IAP wrapper, including the critical server-side receipt validation step that prevents fraud, and design a monetization framework that generates revenue while respecting the player.
key_concepts: Premium model: one-time purchase, no additional monetization, relies on volume sales and marketing
Freemium model: free download, revenue from IAP (consumables, cosmetics, battlepasses, subscriptions)
Ad-supported model: rewarded video (player opts in for reward), interstitial (forced between sessions), banner (persistent low-revenue)
Hybrid model: combining IAP with ads, ad removal as a purchase, rewarded ads as a retention mechanic
IAP types: consumable (currency packs), non-consumable (permanent unlocks), auto-renewing subscriptions
Server-side receipt validation: verifying purchases with Apple/Google servers to prevent tampered receipts and refund fraud
ARPDAU: Average Revenue Per Daily Active User, the primary metric for comparing monetization effectiveness
LTV: Lifetime Value = ARPDAU x average player lifetime in days, must exceed CPI for profitability
Conversion funnel: free player > first session > day-1 return > first purchase > recurring spender, with drop-off at each stage
Ethical monetization: disclosed odds for randomized rewards, no pay-to-win in competitive modes, age-gating for purchases, spending limits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a complete IAP flow in Unity: configuring products in Apple App Store Connect and Google Play Console, using Unity IAP to present a storefront, handling the purchase flow with loading states and error handling, validating receipts server-side using a Firebase Cloud Function that calls Apple and Google verification APIs, and restoring purchases on reinstall. Testing the full flow in sandbox mode.
setup_requirements: Unity IAP package configured for both iOS and Android
Apple Developer account with App Store Connect sandbox tester
Google Play Console with internal testing track configured
Firebase project for server-side receipt validation function

review.value: Verifying purchase receipts with Apple/Google servers from your own backend rather than trusting the client. Client-side validation alone is insecure because the app binary can be modified to report fake purchases. Server validation is mandatory for any real monetization.

Total daily revenue divided by daily active users. A healthy F2P game targets $0.05-0.15 ARPDAU for casual games, $0.15-0.50 for mid-core. Tracks the effectiveness of monetization independent of audience size.

The percentage of players who make at least one purchase. Typical F2P games see 2-5% conversion. The first purchase has the highest friction; subsequent purchases convert at higher rates. First-purchase offers and starter packs optimize this step.

Players voluntarily watch a 15-30 second video ad in exchange for an in-game reward (currency, extra life, power-up). Generates $10-40 eCPM, higher than any other ad format, and players perceive it as a fair exchange rather than an interruption.

learning_objectives: Integrate analytics SDKs to track player behavior and business metrics
Design event taxonomies that provide actionable insights for game improvement
Build retention, monetization, and engagement dashboards from tracked events
Use cohort analysis and funnel visualization to identify design problems
readings: Game Analytics: Maximizing the Value of Player Data Ch. 2-5
Firebase Analytics Documentation
Unity Analytics Documentation
GameAnalytics Best Practices Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Analytics is how you know whether your game is succeeding or failing. Without analytics, every design decision is based on intuition. With analytics, you can measure exactly where players quit, which levels are too hard, which items players buy, and whether your latest content update improved retention or hurt it. The mobile game industry runs on data: day-1 retention below 40% means your onboarding is failing, day-7 below 15% means your core loop is not engaging, and LTV below CPI means you are losing money on every player you acquire. Today we integrate Firebase Analytics and design a comprehensive event taxonomy for a mobile game. An event taxonomy is the vocabulary your analytics speaks -- poorly designed events give you noise, while well-designed events give you actionable insights. We track the complete player journey: first open, tutorial completion, first gameplay session, first IAP, and every engagement milestone. We build dashboards that visualize retention curves, conversion funnels, level completion rates, and session length distributions. We then use cohort analysis to compare players who saw the old tutorial against players who saw the new tutorial, demonstrating how analytics drives data-informed design decisions.
key_concepts: Event taxonomy design: naming conventions (verb_noun: level_completed, item_purchased), parameter standards, event hierarchy
Automatic events: first_open, session_start, screen_view collected without code, providing baseline engagement data
Custom events: game-specific tracking for level completion, currency earned/spent, feature usage, error occurrences
User properties: persistent attributes like player_level, subscription_status, preferred_mode for segmenting analytics
Retention metrics: D1, D7, D30 retention rates measured as percentage of cohort returning after N days
Funnel analysis: tracking step-by-step progression through onboarding, purchase flow, or level sequences to identify drop-off points
Cohort analysis: comparing behavior of player groups defined by acquisition date, source, or A/B test variant
Session metrics: session count per day, average session length, time between sessions, session depth (levels per session)
Revenue analytics: ARPDAU, ARPPU (Average Revenue Per Paying User), purchase frequency, average transaction value
Privacy compliance: GDPR consent flows, Apple ATT (App Tracking Transparency), data minimization principles
lecture_notes.demonstration: Integrating Firebase Analytics into a Unity mobile game: initializing the SDK, logging custom events for level_started, level_completed (with parameters: level_id, time_to_complete, deaths, score), item_purchased, and ad_watched. Building a Firebase dashboard showing D1/D7 retention, a level completion funnel, and revenue by country. Then implementing Apple's ATT consent dialog and GDPR consent flow for EU users.
setup_requirements: Firebase project with Analytics enabled
Firebase Unity SDK installed in the project
Apple Developer account for ATT consent testing
Sample game with multiple levels and an IAP store

review.value: The percentage of players who return to the game the day after first install. Industry benchmark: 35-45% for casual, 25-35% for mid-core. Below 30% typically indicates problems with the onboarding or first session experience.

Visualizing the drop-off at each step of a multi-step process. If 1000 players start the tutorial but only 400 complete it, the 60% drop-off identifies the tutorial as a problem area worth redesigning.

Apple's iOS 14.5+ requirement to request user permission before tracking across apps. Affects attribution and ad targeting. Must be shown before any tracking occurs. Denial rates are typically 70-80%.

Grouping players by their install date (or other attribute) and comparing their behavior over time. Reveals whether game updates improve or hurt metrics for new players, independent of the existing player base.

learning_objectives: Design push notification strategies that re-engage lapsed players without causing uninstalls
Implement local and remote push notifications on iOS and Android
Build engagement systems: daily rewards, streak bonuses, and time-limited events
Measure notification effectiveness with open rates, return rates, and uninstall correlation
readings: Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products Ch. 3-5 (game context)
Apple Push Notification Service Documentation
Firebase Cloud Messaging Documentation
Mobile Engagement Best Practices (Leanplum, 2024)

lecture_notes.introduction: A player who does not return to your game will never spend money in it, watch an ad, or recommend it to friends. Re-engagement is the most valuable action in mobile gaming, and push notifications are the primary tool for driving it. But notifications are a double-edged sword: well-timed, relevant notifications bring players back; poorly timed, irrelevant notifications drive them to disable notifications or uninstall entirely. The difference between a notification that says 'Come back and play!' and one that says 'Your troops have finished training and the alliance war starts in 2 hours' is the difference between spam and service. Today we implement both local notifications (scheduled by the game client for predictable events like timer completions, energy refills, and daily reward resets) and remote notifications (sent from a server to target specific player segments based on behavior). We also build the in-game engagement systems that create reasons to return: daily login rewards with escalating value and streak bonuses, time-limited events with unique rewards, and social features that create obligations (alliance activities, gift exchanges). We measure notification effectiveness rigorously: opt-in rate, open rate, return rate after notification, session depth after notification-driven return, and critically, the correlation between notification frequency and uninstall rate.
key_concepts: Local notifications: scheduled by the app for predictable events (timer complete, energy full, daily reward available)
Remote notifications: sent from a server via APNS/FCM targeting specific player segments based on behavior data
Notification permission: iOS requires explicit opt-in (ask at the right moment, not on first launch), Android 13+ requires permission
Notification content: specific and relevant ('Your farm is ready to harvest') beats generic ('Come back and play!')
Optimal timing: sending during the player's typical play window, respecting quiet hours, limiting to 2-3 per week maximum
Daily reward systems: escalating rewards over consecutive days with a streak bonus, reset after missed days (or partial reset)
Time-limited events: creating urgency with exclusive rewards available only during a window (weekend events, seasonal content)
Social engagement hooks: alliance activities, gift systems, leaderboards, and cooperative goals that create return obligations
Notification A/B testing: testing message content, timing, and frequency against control groups to optimize open rates
Uninstall correlation: monitoring whether notification frequency increases uninstall rates, finding the optimal frequency ceiling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a complete notification system: local notifications for daily reward availability and energy refill, remote notifications via Firebase Cloud Messaging targeting players who have not played in 3 days, an in-game daily reward calendar UI with streak tracking, and a time-limited weekend event with exclusive cosmetic rewards. Setting up A/B testing for notification timing using Firebase Remote Config.
setup_requirements: Firebase Cloud Messaging configured for both iOS and Android
Unity Mobile Notifications package installed
Firebase Remote Config for A/B testing notification parameters
Sample game with energy system and daily reward calendar

review.value: Requesting notification permission on first launch yields 40-50% opt-in. Requesting after the player experiences a timer or reward they would want to be notified about yields 60-70% opt-in. Always show a pre-permission screen explaining the benefit before the system dialog.

Rewards escalate over consecutive days (Day 1: 100 coins, Day 7: rare item). Missing a day resets the streak, creating motivation to return daily. Some designs offer partial reset (back 2 days) to be less punishing.

Players who receive more than 3-5 notifications per week show increased uninstall rates. The optimal frequency depends on genre: casual games tolerate fewer notifications than strategy games where timers and wars create legitimate reasons for alerts.

Sending different notification content to different player segments: lapsed players get re-engagement messages, active non-spenders get value proposition messages, spenders get new content announcements. One-size-fits-all messaging underperforms segmented approaches by 40-60%.

learning_objectives: Apply ASO techniques to maximize organic discoverability on app stores
Create compelling store listings that convert impressions to installs
Conduct keyword research and competitive analysis for app store search ranking
Plan soft-launch and phased rollout strategies
readings: App Store Optimization: The Ultimate Guide (2024 Edition)
Apple App Store Review Guidelines Section 2 - Performance
Google Play Store Listing Guidelines
Sensor Tower ASO Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: You can build the best mobile game in the world, but if nobody finds it on the app store, it will fail. App Store Optimization is the discipline of making your game discoverable and compelling to potential players. ASO is to mobile games what SEO is to websites -- it determines whether your game appears in search results when players look for games in your genre. The two major app stores (Apple App Store and Google Play Store) each have their own search algorithms, but both weigh similar factors: keyword relevance in title and metadata, install velocity (how many installs per time period), user ratings and review sentiment, retention and engagement metrics, and conversion rate (impressions to install page views to installs). Today we cover the complete ASO workflow: researching keywords using tools like App Annie and Sensor Tower, crafting a title and subtitle that balance branding with keyword inclusion, writing a description that converts readers to installers, creating screenshots and preview videos that showcase gameplay (not menus), and managing ratings and reviews. We also discuss soft-launch strategy: releasing in a limited market (typically Canada, Australia, or Philippines) to gather real player data on retention and monetization before committing to a worldwide launch with marketing spend.
key_concepts: Keyword research: identifying search terms with high volume and low competition using ASO tools
Title optimization: 30 characters on iOS, 50 on Android, must include primary keyword and game name
Subtitle/short description: secondary keywords and value proposition in 30 characters (iOS) or 80 characters (Android)
Screenshot optimization: first 2-3 screenshots drive most conversion, must show gameplay with benefit-oriented captions
Preview video: 15-30 second gameplay trailer that auto-plays on the store page, must capture attention in first 3 seconds
Ratings and reviews management: in-app review prompts timed after positive experiences, responding to negative reviews
Localized store listings: translating and adapting metadata and screenshots for each target market
Soft-launch methodology: limited market release (1-3 countries), KPI thresholds for retention and monetization, iteration before global launch
A/B testing store listings: Google Play Experiments and Apple Product Page Optimization for testing screenshots and descriptions
Conversion rate optimization: measuring and improving the funnel from store impression to install page view to install
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting keyword research for a puzzle game using Sensor Tower: finding keywords with 40+ search volume and under 30 competition score, crafting an optimized title and subtitle, creating a store listing with gameplay screenshots that include benefit captions ('200+ Handcrafted Puzzles'), and setting up a Google Play Store Listing Experiment to A/B test two screenshot sets against each other.
setup_requirements: App Store Connect and Google Play Console access
ASO tool access (Sensor Tower free tier or App Annie)
Figma or equivalent for screenshot creation with device frames
Screen recording tool for preview video capture

review.value: Releasing in a small market (e.g., Canada, 3% of US market size) to measure retention and monetization with real players before investing in marketing. If D1 retention is below 40% and D7 below 15%, iterate before launching globally.

The percentage of users who visit the store page and install. Average is 25-35% on iOS, 20-30% on Play Store. Optimized through compelling screenshots, clear value proposition, and strong ratings (4.0+ stars).

Overloading metadata with keywords at the expense of readability. Both stores penalize this. The title should read naturally while including primary keywords. Keyword density matters less than relevance and download velocity.

Using SKStoreReviewController (iOS) or Google In-App Review API (Android) to prompt players to rate the game. Timing matters: prompt after a positive experience (level completion, reward collection), not during frustration or on first launch.

learning_objectives: Architect games for cross-platform deployment from a single codebase
Implement platform abstraction layers for input, rendering, and services
Evaluate cross-platform frameworks: Unity, Unreal, Godot, Flutter, React Native for games
Handle platform-specific code paths with conditional compilation and dependency injection
readings: Cross-Platform Game Development with Unity Ch. 8-10
Unity Platform-Dependent Compilation Documentation
Design Patterns: Service Locator and Dependency Injection

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern mobile games rarely exist on a single platform. Publishing on both iOS and Android is the minimum, and many games extend to PC, console, and web. Cross-platform development from a single codebase is economically necessary -- maintaining separate iOS and Android codebases doubles your development cost, bug surface, and maintenance burden. Unity and Unreal Engine are the dominant cross-platform solutions for games, handling the heavy lifting of rendering, audio, and input abstraction. But platform differences still leak through: IAP flows differ between Apple StoreKit and Google Play Billing, push notification registration differs between APNS and FCM, and even basic features like haptic feedback and file system access have platform-specific APIs. Today we architect a platform abstraction layer that isolates all platform-specific code behind common interfaces, allowing the game logic to interact with a single set of service interfaces while concrete implementations handle the platform differences. We use the service locator pattern at application startup to register the appropriate implementation for each platform, and conditional compilation for the small number of cases where even the registration code must differ.
key_concepts: Platform abstraction layer: interfaces for IAP, ads, analytics, notifications, haptics, cloud saves
Service locator pattern: a central registry mapping interface types to platform-specific concrete implementations
Dependency injection: providing platform implementations to game systems through constructors or properties
Conditional compilation: #if UNITY_IOS / #if UNITY_ANDROID for platform-specific code paths
Unity's platform defines: UNITY_IOS, UNITY_ANDROID, UNITY_WEBGL, UNITY_STANDALONE, UNITY_EDITOR
Cross-platform input: Unity Input System actions mapped to touch, gamepad, and keyboard transparently
Rendering scalability: quality settings profiles per platform with different resolution, effects, and draw distance
Asset variants: platform-specific texture compression (ASTC for mobile, BC7 for PC) via import settings
Build automation: scripting build pipelines that produce iOS, Android, and PC builds from a single project
Testing across platforms: device farms (AWS Device Farm, Firebase Test Lab) for automated testing on many devices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a platform abstraction layer with three services: IAPService, AnalyticsService, and HapticsService. Implementing AppleIAPService and GoogleIAPService concrete classes, registering them in a ServiceLocator at startup based on platform, then showing the game store UI calling ServiceLocator.Get<IAPService>().PurchaseProduct() without knowing which platform it is running on. Demonstrating the same game building and running on Android and iOS from the single codebase.
setup_requirements: Unity project with both iOS and Android build targets
Interface-based service architecture scaffolded
Both Apple and Google IAP SDKs configured in the project
Build automation script for multi-platform builds

review.value: A central object that maps interface types to concrete implementations. At startup: ServiceLocator.Register<IAPService>(new AppleIAPService()) on iOS. Game code: ServiceLocator.Get<IAPService>().Purchase(). Platform-specific code is isolated in registration and implementation classes.

Code blocks wrapped in #if UNITY_IOS ... #elif UNITY_ANDROID ... #endif are only compiled for the target platform. Useful for platform-specific imports and SDK initialization, but should not be scattered throughout game logic.

Unity import settings can specify different compression formats per platform. A texture can be ASTC 6x6 on iOS, ASTC 6x6 on Android, and BC7 on PC, all configured in a single import settings panel without duplicating the source asset.

Cloud services that provide access to hundreds of real physical devices for automated testing. Firebase Test Lab runs your APK on dozens of Android devices and reports crashes, screenshots, and performance data without requiring you to own each device.

learning_objectives: Design live operations systems for ongoing content delivery and player engagement
Implement remote configuration for live tuning without app updates
Build a content delivery pipeline using addressable assets for hot content updates
Plan live event schedules and seasonal content cadences
readings: Live Ops Handbook: Running Games as a Service Ch. 1-5
Firebase Remote Config Documentation
Unity Addressables Documentation - Content Update Workflow
GDC Vault: Live Operations Lessons from Clash Royale

lecture_notes.introduction: A mobile game is never finished at launch. The most successful mobile games operate as live services, releasing new content, running time-limited events, and tuning game balance continuously for years after initial release. Live operations is the practice of keeping the game fresh, profitable, and growing after launch. Remote configuration allows you to change game parameters -- difficulty curves, IAP prices, event schedules, feature flags -- without shipping an app update through the store review process. Addressable assets enable downloading new content (levels, characters, cosmetics) from a CDN without an app update. Feature flags enable gradual rollouts and A/B testing of new features. Together, these systems transform a static shipped product into a dynamic service that can respond to player behavior in hours instead of weeks. Today we implement the complete live ops technology stack: Firebase Remote Config for real-time parameter tuning, Unity Addressables for downloadable content with versioning and caching, feature flags for controlled rollouts, and a time-limited event system that activates automatically based on server time. We also discuss the operational cadence: how often to release content, when to run events, and how to plan a content calendar that keeps players engaged month after month.
key_concepts: Remote configuration: server-controlled key-value pairs that override local defaults, fetched and activated at app start
Feature flags: boolean remote config values that enable/disable features for all users or specific segments
A/B testing with remote config: randomly assigning players to groups that see different values for the same parameter
Addressable content updates: uploading new asset bundles to a CDN, updating the content catalog, and having the game download them
Content versioning: tracking which content version each player has and serving appropriate updates
Live event system: server-time-driven events with start/end timestamps, exclusive rewards, and event-specific gameplay
Seasonal content: holiday-themed events, battle passes with tiered rewards, and limited-time cosmetics that drive FOMO
Emergency kill switches: feature flags that can instantly disable a broken feature without an app update
Content calendar planning: scheduling events, content drops, and updates across a 3-6 month rolling window
Graceful offline handling: caching last-known remote config, using local defaults when server is unreachable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a complete live ops pipeline: configuring Firebase Remote Config with default values for difficulty_multiplier, event_active, and event_end_date; implementing fetch-and-activate on app start with 12-hour cache expiry; creating an Addressable content group with a seasonal character skin hosted on Firebase Hosting; building a time-limited event UI that appears/disappears based on server timestamps from Remote Config; and demonstrating an emergency kill switch that disables a feature across all players within minutes.
setup_requirements: Firebase project with Remote Config and Hosting configured
Unity Addressables package with remote content catalog
CDN hosting for addressable content bundles
Sample game with configurable difficulty and event system

review.value: Fetch (download from server) > Activate (apply to runtime) > Use (read values). Fetched values do not take effect until activated, allowing you to control the exact moment changes apply. Default values provide fallback when offline.

Server-controlled booleans that gate feature visibility. Enable a new feature for 5% of users first, monitor for crashes, then ramp to 100%. If a bug is found, disable instantly without an app update.

A JSON manifest listing all addressable assets, their locations (local or remote), and their content hashes. The game downloads the catalog at startup and uses it to resolve asset addresses to download URLs.

A seasonal progression system with free and premium reward tracks. Players earn progress through daily/weekly challenges. Premium track requires purchase ($5-10). Resets each season (4-8 weeks). Drives both engagement and revenue.

learning_objectives: Design and execute mobile-specific test plans covering interruptions, permissions, and edge cases
Test across device configurations using physical devices and cloud device farms
Validate app lifecycle behavior: backgrounding, memory warnings, low storage, and connectivity loss
Implement automated UI testing for mobile game flows
readings: Mobile Game Testing Best Practices Guide
Firebase Test Lab Documentation
Apple Xcode XCTest UI Testing Documentation
Android Espresso Testing Documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile testing goes far beyond 'does the game work.' Mobile devices exist in an environment of constant interruption: phone calls, notifications, low battery warnings, OS updates, and connectivity changes. Your game must handle all of these gracefully. A player in the middle of a boss fight receives a phone call. When they return 3 minutes later, the game must resume exactly where they left off. The phone runs low on memory and the OS sends a memory warning. Your game must reduce its footprint without crashing. The player enters a subway tunnel and loses cellular connectivity. Your game must handle the loss of network without corrupting save data or losing progress. These scenarios are not edge cases -- they are the reality of every mobile gaming session. Today we design a comprehensive mobile test plan that covers interruption handling, memory pressure, storage exhaustion, connectivity transitions, permission revocation, OS version compatibility, and accessibility features. We execute these tests on physical devices and automate them using cloud device farms. We also implement automated UI tests that verify the critical path through our game's menus and purchase flows, running them on every build in CI.
key_concepts: Interruption testing: phone calls, alarms, notifications, multitasking, control center/notification shade overlay
App lifecycle: onPause/onResume (Android), applicationWillResignActive/didBecomeActive (iOS), save state before background
Memory pressure testing: simulating low-memory conditions and verifying the game reduces footprint without crashing
Connectivity testing: WiFi to cellular handoff, airplane mode, intermittent connectivity, offline gameplay
Permission testing: camera, microphone, notification, tracking permissions granted, denied, and revoked after initial grant
Device farm testing: running automated test suites on 20-50 real devices via Firebase Test Lab or AWS Device Farm
OS version compatibility: testing on minimum supported OS version and latest version, verifying no deprecated API crashes
Screen size regression: verifying UI layout on smallest supported phone, largest tablet, and foldable devices
Automated UI testing: XCTest (iOS) and Espresso (Android) for scripted interaction flows and screenshot comparison
Crash reporting: Firebase Crashlytics integration for real-time crash monitoring with stack traces and device metadata
lecture_notes.demonstration: Executing a mobile test plan on a physical device: verifying save state persistence through a backgrounding cycle, testing IAP flow interruption (kill the app mid-purchase and verify pending transaction recovery on relaunch), simulating airplane mode during a network request and verifying graceful error handling, revoking notification permission in Settings and verifying the game does not crash on notification send, and setting up Firebase Test Lab to run a Robo test crawl on 20 different Android devices with crash and screenshot reporting.
setup_requirements: Physical iOS and Android test devices
Firebase Crashlytics and Test Lab configured
Network conditioning tools (Network Link Conditioner on iOS, Toxiproxy on Android)
Automated test scripts for critical user flows

review.value: When the OS signals the app is going to background (onPause/applicationWillResignActive), immediately save the game state to persistent storage. The OS may terminate the app at any time after backgrounding without further notice.

If an IAP is interrupted (app crash, network loss, kill), the purchase may have been charged but the game did not deliver the item. On relaunch, check for pending transactions via StoreKit/Play Billing and complete delivery.

Real-time crash reporting SDK that captures stack traces, device info, and breadcrumbs for every crash. Provides crash-free user percentage (target: 99.5%+), groups crashes by root cause, and alerts on new crash patterns.

Firebase Test Lab's automated crawler that explores an app by tapping UI elements, taking screenshots, and logging crashes. Runs on multiple devices simultaneously. Useful for finding crashes on untested device configurations without writing test scripts.

learning_objectives: Prepare app store submissions that pass review on the first attempt
Navigate Apple App Store Review Guidelines and Google Play policies
Handle common rejection reasons and appeal processes
Manage release channels: internal testing, closed beta, open beta, and production
readings: Apple App Store Review Guidelines (2024)
Google Play Developer Program Policies
Common App Store Rejection Reasons (AppFollow Report, 2024)
TestFlight and Google Play Console Internal Testing Documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: The app store review process is the final gate between your game and players. Apple reviews every app update submitted to the App Store, with a typical turnaround of 24-48 hours. Google Play uses a combination of automated scanning and human review. Both platforms have extensive guidelines covering content ratings, privacy policies, monetization disclosures, and technical requirements. Failing review delays your launch and can cost weeks if the rejection requires significant changes. The most common rejection reasons are avoidable: missing privacy policy URL, inadequate login information for review, undisclosed data collection, broken links, and crashes during review. Today we walk through the complete submission process for both platforms: configuring app metadata, preparing review information, building and uploading the binary, managing release channels (internal testing, TestFlight/closed beta, production), and handling rejections with effective appeals. We also cover age ratings (ESRB/IARC), content moderation requirements for user-generated content, and the specific disclosure requirements for loot boxes and randomized purchase mechanics in various jurisdictions.
key_concepts: App Store Connect submission: build upload via Xcode, metadata completion, review information, phased release
Google Play Console submission: AAB upload, release tracks (internal, closed, open, production), managed publishing
Review information: demo accounts, feature descriptions, notes for reviewers explaining non-obvious functionality
Privacy policy requirements: URL required by both platforms, must accurately describe data collection and usage
Content ratings: IARC questionnaire on Google Play, manual rating on App Store, legal requirements vary by country
In-app purchase disclosure: loot box odds disclosure required in many jurisdictions (China, Belgium, Japan)
Age gating: COPPA compliance for under-13 users (US), age verification requirements in various markets
Rejection handling: reading the rejection reason carefully, making the minimum required change, and resubmitting with review notes
Phased rollout: releasing to 1% > 5% > 20% > 50% > 100% of users over days, monitoring crash rates at each stage
Emergency hotfix process: expedited review requests on iOS, staged rollout halt on Android, rollback procedures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a complete Apple App Store submission: uploading a build from Xcode, filling in metadata (description, keywords, screenshots, preview video), configuring age rating, setting up the privacy questionnaire, providing review notes and demo credentials, and submitting for review. Then doing the same for Google Play Console: uploading an AAB, configuring the store listing, setting up the content rating questionnaire, and releasing to the internal testing track for team verification before promoting to production.
setup_requirements: Apple Developer account with App Store Connect access
Google Play Developer account with Play Console access
Signed iOS build (.ipa) and Android App Bundle (.aab)
Privacy policy hosted at a public URL
Store listing assets: screenshots, preview video, icon

review.value: Apple: typically 24-48 hours for initial review, faster for subsequent updates. Google: 1-7 days for new apps, usually within hours for updates. Expedited review is available on iOS for critical bug fixes.

Crashes during review, missing privacy policy, requesting unnecessary permissions, misleading screenshots, undisclosed subscriptions, and broken or non-functional features. All are avoidable with thorough pre-submission testing.

Gradually releasing an update to increasing percentages of users over 7 days. If crash rates spike at 1% rollout, halt and fix before reaching more users. Both platforms support this feature for production releases.

Google Play requires Android App Bundle (AAB) format, not APK. AAB lets Google Play generate optimized APKs per device (correct architecture, screen density, language). Reduces download size by 15-20% compared to universal APK.

learning_objectives: Monitor live game health using crash reports, analytics, and user feedback
Plan and execute update cadences that maintain player engagement
Respond to negative reviews and community feedback constructively
Analyze post-launch data to prioritize feature development and content creation
readings: Running Game Studios: Everything You Need to Know Ch. 12-14
App Annie State of Mobile Gaming (latest annual report)
Post-Launch Game Analytics: Lessons from 100 Mobile Games (GDC Vault)

lecture_notes.introduction: Launch day is not the finish line -- it is the starting line. The most successful mobile games generate the majority of their lifetime revenue in the months and years after launch, not during the launch window. Post-launch management is the ongoing practice of monitoring game health, responding to player feedback, fixing bugs, releasing content updates, and iterating on game design based on real player data. Your analytics dashboard becomes your primary tool: retention curves tell you whether players are staying, revenue metrics tell you whether monetization is working, crash reports tell you what is broken, and review sentiment tells you what players think. Today we study the post-launch workflow: setting up real-time monitoring dashboards that alert you to anomalies (crash rate spike, revenue drop, retention change), establishing an update cadence (weekly bug fixes, biweekly content updates, monthly features), managing the community through review responses and social media, and using player data to prioritize what to build next. We also discuss the difficult decision of when to sunset a game: the metrics that indicate a game has reached the end of its profitable lifecycle, and the respectful way to shut down a live service.
key_concepts: Live monitoring dashboard: crash-free rate, DAU, D1/D7/D30 retention, ARPDAU, session metrics, all with alerting thresholds
Crash triage: prioritizing crashes by frequency and severity, fixing top-3 crashes within 24 hours of detection
Update cadence: weekly hotfixes for critical bugs, biweekly content drops, monthly feature releases, quarterly major updates
Review management: responding to negative reviews within 24 hours, addressing specific complaints, updating response when fixed
Community management: official social media presence, Discord/Reddit engagement, transparent communication about known issues
Data-driven prioritization: using retention data to identify which features to improve, funnel data to find design problems
User acquisition re-evaluation: adjusting CPI targets and channel mix based on actual LTV data from launched game
Competitor monitoring: tracking competitor updates, new entrants, and market shifts that affect your game's position
Sunset planning: declining DAU, negative ROI on updates, graceful shutdown with player communication and refund policies
Post-mortem: honest team reflection on what went right, what went wrong, and what to do differently on the next project
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a post-launch monitoring setup: Firebase Crashlytics dashboard with crash-free rate alerts below 99.5%, Firebase Analytics dashboard with DAU and retention trend visualization, App Store Connect and Play Console review monitoring with sentiment analysis, and a prioritization spreadsheet that maps analytics findings to development tasks ranked by expected impact on retention and revenue.
setup_requirements: Firebase Crashlytics and Analytics dashboards configured
App Store Connect and Google Play Console with a published app
Slack or email integration for crash rate and anomaly alerts
Spreadsheet template for data-driven prioritization

review.value: The percentage of user sessions that do not experience a crash. Target: 99.5% or higher. A rate below 99% indicates a critical stability problem. Firebase Crashlytics tracks this automatically and can alert on drops.

Respond to 1-star and 2-star reviews within 24 hours with empathy and specific action. 'We are aware of this crash on Samsung devices and a fix is in our next update (v2.1.3).' Players who see their issue acknowledged often update their review.

Rank development tasks by expected impact on key metrics. If D1 retention is low, prioritize onboarding improvements. If monetization conversion is low, prioritize shop UX. If crash rate is high, prioritize stability. Data determines what to build next.

Too-frequent mandatory updates annoy players, especially large downloads on cellular. Keep mandatory updates to critical fixes. Deliver content updates via remote config and addressable assets without requiring store updates.

learning_objectives: Present a complete mobile game published to a test track with all course systems integrated
Demonstrate analytics, monetization, live ops, and performance optimization in a real product
Articulate business and design decisions with supporting data
Evaluate peer projects against mobile industry standards
readings: Game Postmortem Writing Guide
Mobile Game Pitch Deck Best Practices

lecture_notes.introduction: Today each student presents their final project: a complete mobile game published to a test track (TestFlight or Google Play Internal Testing) with integrated analytics, monetization, live operations, and performance optimization. Your presentation is a professional pitch that demonstrates not just your technical skills but your understanding of the mobile game business. You should be prepared to show live analytics data from real test sessions, demonstrate your IAP flow working in sandbox mode, show your remote config updating game parameters in real time, present your store listing with optimized metadata, and discuss your performance profiling results on target mobile hardware. This is the closest simulation to a real mobile game launch that an academic setting can provide. Your post-mortem should reflect honestly on what worked, what did not, and what you learned about the unique challenges and opportunities of mobile game development. The mobile game industry values execution over ideas -- today you demonstrate execution.
key_concepts: Live demo on device: showing the game running on a real phone, not an emulator or editor
Analytics walkthrough: presenting real data from test sessions showing retention, engagement, and monetization events
Monetization demonstration: completing a sandbox IAP purchase and showing receipt validation in server logs
Remote config live change: modifying a parameter in Firebase console and showing it update in the running game
Performance evidence: profiler data showing frame time, memory usage, and thermal behavior on target hardware
Store listing review: presenting the optimized app store page with screenshots, description, and keyword analysis
Post-mortem analysis: structured reflection on development process, technical decisions, and business strategy
Peer evaluation: constructive feedback on UX, performance, and monetization approach
Portfolio presentation: framing the project for job applications in mobile game studios
Industry readiness: demonstrating understanding of the end-to-end mobile game lifecycle
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student presents for 8-10 minutes with 3-5 minutes of Q&A. Presentations include a live game demo on a physical device, a walkthrough of analytics dashboards with real data, and a post-mortem discussion of key decisions and lessons learned.
setup_requirements: Physical mobile device with the final game installed from a test track
Firebase Analytics and Remote Config dashboards accessible on a laptop
Presentation slides with performance profiling data and store listing screenshots
Written post-mortem document submitted before presentation

review.value: The game must be published to TestFlight (iOS) or Google Play Internal Testing (Android) to demonstrate the ability to build, sign, upload, and distribute a real mobile application through official channels.
Real data from test sessions displayed in Firebase Analytics dashboards: session counts, event logs, retention calculation from multiple test days, and monetization events from sandbox purchases.
Presenting the game as if pitching to a publisher or studio: clear value proposition, target audience, monetization strategy with expected metrics, competitive analysis, and development roadmap.

The best post-mortems are honest about failures. A student who acknowledges that their monetization design was too aggressive or their retention was below target and explains what they would change demonstrates more learning than one who claims everything went perfectly.

quizzes.topic: Mobile Platforms, Touch Input, and Mobile UI

questions.question: A mobile game runs at a stable 60fps for the first 10 minutes of play, then gradually drops to 35fps over the next 20 minutes with no change in scene complexity. The device surface is noticeably warm. Explain the most likely cause and describe two concrete strategies the developer should implement to address this problem.
questions.answer: The most likely cause is thermal throttling. As the device heats up from sustained GPU/CPU load, the operating system reduces clock speeds to prevent hardware damage, resulting in lower frame rates despite constant scene complexity. Strategy 1: Implement an adaptive quality system that monitors device thermal state (using iOS ProcessInfo.thermalState or Android PowerManager) and dynamically reduces rendering resolution, shadow quality, or particle counts when thermal pressure increases. Strategy 2: Reduce sustained GPU load proactively by targeting 70-80% GPU utilization rather than maximum. Use lower render resolution (85-90% with upscaling), implement more aggressive LOD transitions, and ensure shaders avoid expensive operations like dependent texture reads in fragment shaders.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies thermal throttling as the cause
First strategy is concrete with specific technical approach
Second strategy is concrete with specific technical approach

questions.question: Explain the difference between a static and dynamic virtual joystick, and describe when each is the better choice. Include the role of dead zones in both designs.
questions.answer: A static virtual joystick is always visible at a fixed screen position. The player must reach to the joystick's location to use it. A dynamic virtual joystick appears wherever the player first touches the screen and centers itself at that touch point, disappearing when the finger lifts. Static is better for games requiring precise, sustained directional control (fighting games, twin-stick shooters) because the player develops muscle memory for the joystick location. Dynamic is better for casual games and situations where different players hold the device differently, because it accommodates any hand position and does not permanently occupy screen space. Dead zones apply to both: a circular area at the joystick center (10-15% of radius) where input is ignored. Without a dead zone, even resting a thumb on a static joystick causes drift because the thumb is never perfectly centered.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes static and dynamic joystick behaviors
Appropriate use case for each type with reasoning
Explains dead zone purpose and typical radius

questions.question: A mobile game's UI buttons are 30x30 dp on Android. A playtester reports that they frequently miss buttons during fast-paced gameplay. Identify the problem, cite the relevant guideline, and explain the fix.
questions.answer: The buttons are below the minimum recommended touch target size. Android Material Design guidelines specify a minimum of 48x48 dp for touch targets, and Apple Human Interface Guidelines specify 44x44 points. At 30x30 dp, the buttons are approximately 60% of the minimum size, making them unreliable to tap, especially during fast-paced gameplay where the player cannot carefully aim their finger. Fix: Increase the touch target to at least 48x48 dp. If the visual button must remain small for design reasons, extend the invisible touch area (hit rect) beyond the visual bounds while keeping the visual element at its designed size.

rubric.key: Identifies buttons as below minimum touch target size
Cites correct guideline values (48dp Android or 44pt iOS)
Provides appropriate fix including hit area extension option

quizzes.topic: Performance Budgets, Rendering Optimization, and Monetization

questions.question: A free-to-play mobile game has Day 1 retention of 40%, Day 7 retention of 15%, Day 30 retention of 5%, and an ARPDAU of $0.08. Calculate the approximate LTV and explain whether the game can profitably acquire users at a CPI of $2.00.
questions.answer: Using the area-under-the-curve method with the given retention points and assuming a roughly exponential decay, the estimated average player lifetime is approximately 15-20 days. LTV = ARPDAU x average lifetime = $0.08 x 17 days = approximately $1.36. At a CPI of $2.00, the game cannot profitably acquire users because LTV ($1.36) < CPI ($2.00). The LTV/CPI ratio is 0.68, well below the target of 1.5-2.0 that accounts for overhead costs. The developer needs to either improve monetization to raise ARPDAU (better IAP offers, rewarded ads) or improve retention to extend average lifetime (better content, engagement features), or reduce CPI by finding cheaper acquisition channels.

rubric.key: Reasonable LTV calculation using retention and ARPDAU
Correct conclusion that LTV < CPI means unprofitable acquisition
Identifies at least one viable path to profitability

questions.question: Explain why ASTC texture compression is generally preferred over ETC2 for mobile games in 2024-2025, and describe one scenario where ETC2 is still the correct choice.
questions.answer: ASTC is preferred because it offers variable block sizes (4x4 to 12x12 texels) allowing quality-to-size tradeoff per texture, supports both LDR and HDR content, handles all texture types (color, normals, masks) with good quality, and is supported on all modern iOS (A8+) and Android (OpenGL ES 3.2+, Vulkan) devices. ETC2 has fixed 4x4 block size with no quality-size flexibility and produces worse quality for normal maps and gradient-heavy textures. ETC2 is still correct when targeting very old Android devices running OpenGL ES 3.0 that lack ASTC support, as ETC2 is mandatory in the ES 3.0 spec. If your minimum spec includes 2015-era budget phones, ETC2 ensures universal compatibility.

rubric.key: Explains ASTC variable block size and quality advantages
Notes ASTC universal support on modern devices
Valid scenario for choosing ETC2 with reasoning

questions.question: A mobile game has 380 draw calls and is GPU-bound at 22ms/frame on a mid-range phone. Describe three specific rendering optimizations, estimate each one's impact, and explain which mobile GPU characteristic makes draw call reduction especially important.
questions.answer: Optimization 1: Enable SRP Batcher or static batching to combine objects sharing shaders. Estimated impact: reduce draw calls from 380 to ~150, saving 3-5ms from reduced CPU draw call overhead and GPU state changes. Optimization 2: Switch textures from uncompressed to ASTC 6x6 compression. Estimated impact: reduce texture memory bandwidth by 4-8x, saving 2-3ms from reduced GPU memory fetch time. Optimization 3: Enable render scale at 85% and upscale. Estimated impact: reduce fragment count by 28% (0.85^2), saving 2-3ms of fragment shading time. Draw call reduction is especially important on mobile because mobile GPUs use tile-based deferred rendering, where each draw call requires the driver to compute per-tile visibility lists. High draw call counts increase the CPU overhead of tile binning and can cause the driver to become the bottleneck even when the GPU has spare capacity.

rubric.key: Three distinct optimizations with reasonable impact estimates
Estimates total to a reasonable reduction (into budget range)
Correctly explains TBDR tile binning overhead for draw calls

questions.question: A mobile game has D1 retention of 42%, D7 retention of 12%, and D30 retention of 1%. Based on industry benchmarks, diagnose the likely problem area and describe what analytics events you would examine to confirm your diagnosis.
questions.answer: D1 retention of 42% is acceptable (benchmark: 35-45% for casual). The severe drop from D1 (42%) to D7 (12%) and near-total loss by D30 (1%) indicates the core gameplay loop fails to sustain engagement after the initial session. Players try the game and return once, but find insufficient reason to keep playing after the first few days. To confirm, examine: (1) Session depth by day: are D2-D7 sessions getting shorter, indicating declining interest? (2) Level completion funnel: do players hit a wall at a specific level that causes rage-quitting? (3) Content consumption rate: do players exhaust available content within the first week? (4) Feature usage: are engagement features (daily rewards, events, social) being used or ignored? (5) Last action before churn: what is the most common final event before a player never returns?

rubric.key: Correctly identifies core loop engagement as the problem, not onboarding
Names at least three specific analytics events to investigate
Analysis is logical and connects retention pattern to likely causes

questions.question: Explain why requesting push notification permission on first app launch is suboptimal, and describe a better approach with the expected impact on opt-in rates.
questions.answer: Requesting on first launch is suboptimal because the player has no context for why the game needs to send notifications. They have not experienced any game mechanic (timers, events, rewards) that would benefit from notifications. The system dialog offers a binary choice with no explanation, and many players reflexively deny permission for any app they have not yet decided to keep. Expected opt-in rate on first launch: 40-50%. Better approach: Wait until the player completes their first gameplay session and encounters a natural notification trigger -- for example, when they start a timer-based action (building, training, harvesting) that will complete later. Before showing the system dialog, display a custom pre-permission screen that explains the specific benefit: 'Get notified when your crops are ready to harvest so you never lose progress.' Then show the system dialog. Expected opt-in rate with this approach: 60-70%.

rubric.key: Explains why first-launch request lacks context and triggers reflexive denial
Describes a better timing approach with specific trigger example
Mentions pre-permission screen and reasonable opt-in rate improvement

questions.question: What is the purpose of a conversion funnel in analytics, and describe the five stages of a typical mobile game monetization funnel.
questions.answer: A conversion funnel tracks the percentage of users who complete each stage of a multi-step process, revealing where the highest drop-off occurs so developers can focus optimization efforts. Typical mobile game monetization funnel: (1) Free player installs the game (100% baseline). (2) Player reaches the store/shop screen (30-50% of players). (3) Player views a specific IAP offer (15-25% of players). (4) Player initiates a purchase (3-8% of players). (5) Player completes the purchase successfully (2-5% of players, accounting for payment failures and cancellations). The funnel reveals which step loses the most players.
